steps.title: Simplify the relationship between n and k
steps.content:
Start with $4n^5 \cdot 3 = 3k^2$. Divide both sides by $3$:
$$4n^5 = k^2$$
Because $k \gt 1$, take the positive square root:
$$k = 2n^{5/2}$$

steps.title: Use the condition that n equals k
steps.content:
The question asks for $a$ when $n = 2a + 1$ is equal to $k$, so set $n=k$:
$$n = 2n^{5/2}$$
Since $n \gt 1$, divide both sides by $n$:
$$1 = 2n^{3/2}$$
Then $n = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2/3}$, which is less than $1$.

steps.title: Determine whether a valid value exists
steps.content: The value forced by $n=k$ violates the condition $n \gt 1$. Therefore, there is no value of $a$ that satisfies all the stated conditions. The answer is No solution.
